news_summary: NASA’s Artemis II mission advances with its flight around the Moon, while astronomers observe a rare 'dirty fireball' star explosion, marking exciting progress in space exploration and cosmic discoveries.
news_summary_fr: La mission Artemis II de la NASA poursuit son vol autour de la Lune, tandis que les astronomes observent une rare explosion stellaire de type « boule de feu sale », marquant ainsi des avancées passionnantes dans l'exploration spatiale et les découvertes cosmiques.
news_summary_es: La misión Artemis II de la NASA avanza con su vuelo alrededor de la Luna, mientras que los astrónomos observan una inusual explosión estelar conocida como «bolas de fuego sucias», lo que supone un emocionante avance en la exploración espacial y los descubrimientos cósmicos.

articles.title: NASA’s Artemis II Mission Leaves Earth Orbit for Flight around Moon
articles.summary: For the first time in more than 50 years, astronauts on a NASA mission are bound to fly around the Moon after successfully completing a key burn of Orion’s main engine. With the approximately six-minute firing of the spacecraft’s service module engine on Thursday, known as the translunar injection burn, Orion and its crew of […]
articles.content:
Earth’s crescent is seen from a solar array camera on the Orion spacecraft on the first flight day of the Artemis II mission. Credit: NASA

For the first time in more than 50 years, astronauts on a NASA mission are bound to fly around the Moon after successfully completing a key burn of Orion’s main engine.

With the approximately six-minute firing of the spacecraft’s service module engine on Thursday, known as the translunar injection burn, Orion and its crew of NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen accelerated to break free of Earth’s orbit and began the outbound trajectory toward Earth’s nearest neighbor.

“Today, for the first time since Apollo 17 in 1972, humans have departed Earth orbit. Reid, Victor, Christina, and Jeremy now are on a precise trajectory toward the Moon. Orion is operating with crew for the first time in space, and we are gathering critical data, and learning from each step,” said Dr. Lori Glaze, acting associate administrator for the Exploration Systems Development Mission Directorate at NASA Headquarters in Washington. “Each milestone we reach marks meaningful progress on the path forward for the Artemis program. While we have eight intensive days of work ahead, this is a big moment, and we’re proud to share it with the world.”

NASA’s SLS (Space Launch System) rocket and Orion spacecraft lifted off from Launch Pad 39B at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida at 6:35 p.m. EDT on April 1, sending the four astronauts on a planned 10-day test flight around the Moon and back.

After reaching space, Orion deployed its four solar array wings, enabling the spacecraft to receive energy from the Sun, while the crew and engineers on the ground immediately began transitioning the spacecraft from launch to flight operations to start checking out key systems.

About 49 minutes into the test flight, the SLS rocket’s upper stage fired to put Orion into an elliptical orbit around Earth. A second planned burn by the stage propelled Orion, which the crew named “Integrity,” into a high Earth orbit extending about 46,000 miles above the Earth for about 24 hours of system checkouts. After the burn, Orion separated from the stage, flying free on its own.

The crew then conducted a manual piloting demonstration to test Orion’s handling qualities using the ICPS (interim cryogenic propulsion stage) as a docking target.

At the conclusion of the demonstration, Orion executed an automated departure burn to safely back away from the ICPS, after which the stage performed its own disposal burn and re-entered Earth’s atmosphere over a remote region of the Pacific Ocean.

Prior to its re-entry, four small CubeSats were deployed from SLS rocket’s Orion stage adapter.

Other tasks completed so far include a transition to the Deep Space Network for communications, the crew becoming acclimated to the space environment, completing their first rest periods, performing the first flywheel exercise, restoring the spacecraft’s toilet to normal operations, and configuring the spacecraft for the translunar injection burn.

During a planned lunar flyby on Monday, April 6, the astronauts will take high resolution photographs and provide their own observations of the lunar surface, including areas of the far side of the Moon never seen directly by humans. Although the lunar far side will only be partially illuminated during the flyby, the conditions should create shadows that stretch across the surface, enhancing relief and revealing depth, ridges, slopes, and crater rims that are often difficult to detect under full illumination.

Following a successful lunar flyby, the astronauts will return to Earth and splash down in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of San Diego.

As part of a Golden Age of innovation and exploration, NASA will send Artemis astronauts on increasingly challenging missions to explore more of the Moon for scientific discovery, economic benefits, and to build on our foundation for the first crewed missions to Mars.

Follow the latest mission progress, including more images from the test flight, at:

https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii

-end-

Cheryl Warner / Rachel Kraft

Headquarters, Washington

202-358-1600

cheryl.m.warner@nasa.gov / rachel.h.kraft@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant milestone in human space exploration with NASA's Artemis II mission successfully departing Earth orbit for a lunar flyby, the first such crewed mission in over 50 years. This event has broad significance for scientific discovery, technological advancement, and future space exploration including Mars missions. The article provides detailed context about the mission, crew, and objectives, highlighting a meaningful and inspiring achievement.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Artemis II mission begins journey around the Moon after leaving Earth orbit
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA has not sent astronauts to fly around the Moon since 1972. The Artemis II mission is the first crewed trip beyond Earth orbit in over 50 years, aiming to test new spacecraft and prepare for future space exploration.

**What happened** – On April 1, 2024, NASA’s Artemis II spacecraft, called Orion, successfully fired its main engine for about six minutes. This burn, called the translunar injection, pushed Orion and its four astronauts—Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen—out of Earth’s orbit and on a path toward the Moon. The mission launched from Kennedy Space Center in Florida using a powerful rocket called the Space Launch System (SLS).

**Impact** – This is the first time since the Apollo 17 mission that humans have left Earth orbit to travel around the Moon. The mission is testing important spacecraft systems with a crew onboard, including navigation, communications, and life support. It will provide new information about flying near the Moon, which is key for future missions to the Moon’s surface and eventually to Mars. The astronauts will also take detailed photos of parts of the Moon that humans have never seen up close.

**What’s next step** – On April 6, the crew will fly close to the Moon and study its far side, capturing high-resolution images. After completing their lunar flyby, the astronauts will return to Earth and land safely in the Pacific Ocean near San Diego. The data from this mission will help NASA plan longer and more challenging spaceflights in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Artemis II mission marks a major step in human space travel by successfully sending astronauts on a test flight around the Moon for the first time in over 50 years.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Artemis II de la NASA entame son voyage autour de la Lune après avoir quitté l'orbite terrestre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA n’a pas envoyé d’astronautes faire le tour de la Lune depuis 1972. La mission Artemis II est le premier voyage habité au-delà de l’orbite terrestre en plus de 50 ans, visant à tester un nouveau vaisseau spatial et à préparer les futures explorations.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 1er avril 2024, la sonde Artemis II, appelée Orion, a réussi l’allumage principal de son moteur pendant environ six minutes. Cette manœuvre, appelée injection translunaire, a propulsé Orion et ses quatre astronautes—Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch et l’astronaute canadien Jeremy Hansen—hors de l’orbite terrestre en direction de la Lune. La mission a décollé du Kennedy Space Center en Floride avec une fusée puissante appelée Space Launch System (SLS).

**Impact** – C’est la première fois depuis la mission Apollo 17 que des humains quittent l’orbite terrestre pour voyager autour de la Lune. La mission teste des systèmes importants du vaisseau avec un équipage à bord, comme la navigation, les communications et le support de vie. Elle fournira de nouvelles informations sur le vol près de la Lune, cruciales pour les futures missions lunaires et vers Mars. Les astronautes prendront aussi des photos détaillées de parties de la Lune jamais vues de près.

**Prochaine étape** – Le 6 avril, l’équipage s’approchera de la Lune pour étudier sa face cachée et prendre des images haute résolution. Après ce passage autour de la Lune, les astronautes retourneront sur Terre et amerriront en toute sécurité dans l’océan Pacifique près de San Diego. Les données de cette mission aideront la NASA à préparer des vols spatiaux plus longs et plus complexes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA marque une étape majeure en envoyant avec succès des astronautes faire un vol d’essai autour de la Lune pour la première fois en plus de 50 ans.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Artemis II de la NASA inicia su viaje alrededor de la Luna tras salir de la órbita terrestre
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA no ha enviado astronautas a volar alrededor de la Luna desde 1972. La misión Artemis II es el primer viaje tripulado más allá de la órbita terrestre en más de 50 años, con el objetivo de probar una nueva nave espacial y preparar futuras exploraciones.

**Qué sucedió** – El 1 de abril de 2024, la nave Artemis II, llamada Orion, encendió con éxito su motor principal durante unos seis minutos. Esta maniobra, llamada inyección translunar, impulsó a Orion y a sus cuatro astronautas—Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch y el astronauta canadiense Jeremy Hansen—fuera de la órbita terrestre en dirección a la Luna. La misión despegó desde el Centro Espacial Kennedy en Florida usando un cohete potente llamado Sistema de Lanzamiento Espacial (SLS).

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez desde la misión Apollo 17 que humanos salen de la órbita terrestre para viajar alrededor de la Luna. La misión está probando sistemas importantes de la nave con la tripulación a bordo, como navegación, comunicaciones y soporte vital. Proporcionará nueva información sobre vuelos cerca de la Luna, clave para futuras misiones lunares y a Marte. Los astronautas también tomarán fotos detalladas de partes de la Luna que nunca se han visto de cerca.

**Próximo paso** – El 6 de abril, la tripulación se acercará a la Luna para estudiar su lado oculto y tomar imágenes en alta resolución. Después de este sobrevuelo lunar, los astronautas volverán a la Tierra y aterrizarán con seguridad en el océano Pacífico cerca de San Diego. Los datos de esta misión ayudarán a la NASA a planificar vuelos espaciales más largos y difíciles.

**Resumen en una frase** – La misión Artemis II de la NASA marca un paso importante en el viaje espacial humano al enviar con éxito astronautas en un vuelo de prueba alrededor de la Luna por primera vez en más de 50 años.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a sleek, stylized spacecraft with four glowing solar arrays unfolding like wings, silhouetted against a softly glowing crescent Earth in deep space, with a luminous, textured Moon looming ahead; the scene uses warm, natural tones of blues, grays, and soft golds to evoke a hopeful journey beyond Earth’s orbit.

articles.title: We may have seen a 'dirty fireball' star explosion for the first time
articles.summary: An incredibly powerful flash of X-rays spotted by the Einstein Probe telescope appears to be a kind of explosion first theorised more than 30 years ago
articles.content:
An incredibly powerful flash of X-rays spotted by the Einstein Probe telescope appears to be a kind of explosion first theorised more than 30 years ago

Dying stars can emit a powerful jet of radiation, as seen in an artist’s impression Stocktrek Images, Inc./Alamy

Astronomers think they have seen a type of explosion produced by a dying star called a dirty fireball for the first time, and it could help us understand how massive stars die.

When a massive star runs out of fuel, it can collapse and explode in several ways. If a black hole is produced in the collapse, an extremely powerful jet of radiation can burst through the star, producing a flash of high-energy light called a gamma ray burst.

These bursts are among the most powerful explosions in the universe and can emit energy equivalent to the total lifetime output of smaller stars, such as the sun, in a single beam. But astronomers still don’t know exactly how this process works or how variations between different massive stars affect the jet.

Physicists have hypothesised that we might see something different if the jet somehow gets contaminated with heavier matter from the star, such as protons and neutrons. These particles would act as a sponge, slowing the jet down and causing it to emit X-rays, rather than gamma rays. But until now, this “dirty fireball” scenario hadn’t been observed.

Xiang-Yu Wang at Nanjing University in China and his colleagues have now picked up a flash of X-rays called EP241113a that fits the picture of a dirty fireball, using a new space telescope called the Einstein Probe.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

Wang and his team detected a flash of light from a galaxy around 9 billion light years away, containing as much energy as a gamma ray burst, but in X-ray frequencies instead. The initial explosion faded to a glow that lasted several hours, before dying out gradually, similar to a typical gamma ray burst.

“It’s a very exciting prospect,” says Rhaana Starling at the University of Leicester, UK. “[Dirty fireballs] have been theorised to exist since the 90s, but there hasn’t really been any compelling evidence for them.”

While we know of thousands of gamma ray bursts, the event producing this blast is likely to be different from others, says Starling. It might be a black hole or neutron star that is interacting with the jet in an interesting physical way, for example. “If it’s a black hole, then we are able to then get a more complete picture of black hole formation across the universe,” she says.

It also shows us that the gamma ray bursts we typically see could be an observational bias, and there could be many more like this or weaker, says Gavin Lamb at Liverpool John Moores University, UK. “There could well be a continuum that goes right the way down to no jets.”

However, we can’t yet be certain that this is a dirty fireball, says Om Sharan Salafia at Brera Astronomical Observatory in Italy. First, we have to establish whether the explosion really did come from a galaxy as distant as Wang and his team claim. “If all of this holds, then indeed, this transient is a bit puzzling,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the first potential observation of a theorized cosmic event, the 'dirty fireball' star explosion, which advances scientific understanding of massive star deaths and black hole formation. This discovery has broad significance for astrophysics and space science, offering new insights into high-energy cosmic phenomena with substantial real-world impact on knowledge about the universe.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers may have spotted a new type of star explosion called a dirty fireball
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – When very large stars run out of fuel, they can collapse and explode in powerful ways. One known type of explosion shoots out a strong beam of radiation called a gamma ray burst. Scientists have long guessed there might be a different kind of explosion, called a dirty fireball, but no one had seen one before.

**What happened** – Using a new space telescope called the Einstein Probe, scientists detected a bright flash of X-rays from a galaxy 9 billion light years away. This flash had energy like a gamma ray burst but was made of X-rays instead of gamma rays. The event, named EP241113a, faded slowly over hours, matching predictions for a dirty fireball explosion.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it could explain how jets of radiation from dying stars change when mixed with heavier particles like protons and neutrons. Seeing a dirty fireball helps scientists better understand how massive stars collapse and how black holes or neutron stars form. It also suggests there might be many more types of star explosions than we knew.

**What's next step** – Researchers need to confirm that the explosion really came from the distant galaxy as measured. If confirmed, they will study these events more to learn how common dirty fireballs are and how they fit into the bigger picture of star deaths and cosmic explosions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, astronomers may have observed a rare star explosion called a dirty fireball, opening new views on how massive stars die and form black holes.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronomes ont peut-être observé un nouveau type d'explosion d'étoile appelé boule de feu sale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Lorsque de très grandes étoiles manquent de carburant, elles peuvent s'effondrer et exploser de manière puissante. Un type d'explosion connu projette un faisceau fort de rayonnement appelé sursaut gamma. Les scientifiques ont longtemps supposé qu'il existait un autre type d'explosion, appelée boule de feu sale, mais personne ne l'avait jamais observée.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Grâce à un nouveau télescope spatial appelé le Einstein Probe, des scientifiques ont détecté un flash lumineux de rayons X provenant d'une galaxie située à 9 milliards d'années-lumière. Ce flash avait une énergie similaire à celle d'un sursaut gamma, mais était composé de rayons X au lieu de rayons gamma. L'événement, nommé EP241113a, s'est estompé lentement sur plusieurs heures, correspondant aux prévisions d'une explosion de boule de feu sale.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle pourrait expliquer comment les jets de rayonnement des étoiles mourantes changent lorsqu'ils se mélangent à des particules plus lourdes comme des protons et des neutrons. Voir une boule de feu sale aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre comment les étoiles massives s'effondrent et comment se forment les trous noirs ou les étoiles à neutrons. Cela suggère aussi qu'il pourrait y avoir beaucoup plus de types d'explosions d'étoiles que ce que nous connaissions.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs doivent confirmer que l'explosion provient bien de la galaxie lointaine mesurée. Si c'est confirmé, ils étudieront davantage ces événements pour savoir à quelle fréquence les boules de feu sales se produisent et comment elles s'intègrent dans le tableau général des morts d'étoiles et des explosions cosmiques.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, les astronomes ont peut-être observé une explosion d'étoile rare appelée boule de feu sale, ouvrant de nouvelles perspectives sur la mort des étoiles massives et la formation des trous noirs.
articles.personality_title_es: Los astrónomos podrían haber detectado un nuevo tipo de explosión estelar llamada bola de fuego sucia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cuando estrellas muy grandes se quedan sin combustible, pueden colapsar y explotar de manera poderosa. Un tipo conocido de explosión lanza un fuerte haz de radiación llamado estallido de rayos gamma. Los científicos han sospechado por mucho tiempo que podría existir otro tipo de explosión, llamada bola de fuego sucia, pero nadie la había visto antes.

**Qué pasó** – Usando un nuevo telescopio espacial llamado Einstein Probe, los científicos detectaron un brillante destello de rayos X proveniente de una galaxia a 9 mil millones de años luz. Este destello tenía energía similar a un estallido de rayos gamma, pero estaba hecho de rayos X en lugar de rayos gamma. El evento, llamado EP241113a, se desvaneció lentamente durante horas, coincidiendo con las predicciones de una explosión de bola de fuego sucia.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque podría explicar cómo cambian los chorros de radiación de estrellas moribundas cuando se mezclan con partículas más pesadas como protones y neutrones. Ver una bola de fuego sucia ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo colapsan las estrellas masivas y cómo se forman los agujeros negros o estrellas de neutrones. También sugiere que puede haber muchos más tipos de explosiones estelares de lo que se conocía.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores necesitan confirmar que la explosión realmente vino de la galaxia lejana que midieron. Si se confirma, estudiarán más estos eventos para aprender cuán comunes son las bolas de fuego sucias y cómo encajan en el panorama general de la muerte estelar y las explosiones cósmicas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, los astrónomos podrían haber observado una rara explosión estelar llamada bola de fuego sucia, abriendo nuevas formas de ver cómo mueren las estrellas masivas y se forman los agujeros negros.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a distant galaxy softly glowing in warm hues, with an abstract, radiant jet of mixed bright and muted colors bursting outward from a collapsing star at its center, symbolizing a powerful cosmic explosion emitting a mysterious X-ray flash, surrounded by subtle swirling particles representing heavier matter blending into the jet.
